headline: EU releases €3.9bn drone tranche
deck: Brussels on 30 June released €3.9 billion for Ukrainian drone procurement, the first payment in a roughly €6 billion package under the €90 billion support loan. Saab sealed a $2.54 billion Gripen accord the same day. Western capitals are writing expendable autonomy into financed industrial capacity.

art.caption: Europe puts public money behind mass, expendable autonomy

body: On 30 June the European Commission disbursed €3.9 billion for drone procurement to Ukraine, routing the payment through financing tied to its wider €90 billion Ukraine Support Loan [E1][E3].
Ukraine's defence ministry described the transfer as the first tranche of a dedicated €6 billion drone package, signalling serial factory financing ahead of further instalments [E2].

Reuters reported that Commission officials pitched the money to strengthen frontline defence and expand Ukrainian production capacity integrated into Europe's defence-industrial base, a procurement scale large enough to shape supplier winners and battlefield learning loops [E3][E1].
Sweden's Saab signed a $2.54 billion agreement the same day to supply Ukraine with Gripen E fighter jets, a headline procurement that carried a second lane of defence-technology cooperation [E4].

Reuters said President Volodymyr Zelenskiy and Swedish officials also discussed deeper work on drones and missile-defence systems, treating advanced air power and expendable autonomy as a single procurement conversation [E4].
Britain's June Defence Investment Plan, allocating more than £5 billion over four years to drones and autonomous systems, pointed the same direction from London [E6][E7].

Across Brussels, Stockholm and London, governments are writing Ukraine's lesson of mass, cheap, expendable autonomy into budget lines that graduate uncrewed systems from battlefield improvisation into financed industrial architecture [E1][E4][E6].

evidence_box.fragment: first 3.9 billion tranche for drone procurement
